9964: Let T be a topological space. Define the relation \( x \sim y \iff x \text{ and } y \text{ are arc-connected} \). This relation \( \sim \) is an equivalence relation, allowing the partitioning of points in T into equivalence classes. These equivalence classes are called the arc components of T. For \( x \in T \), the arc component containing x is denoted by \( \operatorname{AC}_x T \).

9965: Let T = (S, τ) be a topological space and p ∈ S. Define: A = arc component of p, P = path component of p, C = component of p, Q = quasicomponent of p. Then, A ⊆ P ⊆ C ⊆ Q. In general, the inclusions do not hold in the other direction.

9968: Let K be a number field. The ring of integers of K, denoted \( \mathcal{O}_K \) or \( \mathfrak{o}_K \), is the integral closure of \( \mathbb{Z} \) in K.

9970: A GCD domain is an integral domain where any two non-zero elements have a greatest common divisor.

9973:
Let \( \struct {P, \le} \) be an ordered set. The following are equivalent:

1. Every increasing sequence \( x_1 \le x_2 \le x_3 \le \cdots \) with \( x_i \in P \) eventually terminates: there exists \( n \in \mathbb{N} \) such that \( x_n = x_{n + 1} = \cdots \).

2. Every non-empty subset of \( P \) has a maximal element.

9974: Let R be a commutative ring with unity and M be an R-module. M has the ascending chain condition on submodules if every increasing sequence of submodules N_1 ⊆ N_2 ⊆ N_3 ⊆ ... with N_i in a set D of submodules eventually stabilizes, meaning there exists k in ℕ such that for all n in ℕ, n ≥ k, N_n = N_{n + 1}.

9975:
**Descending Chain Condition on Submodules**: A module satisfies the descending chain condition (DCC) if every descending chain of submodules stabilizes, meaning there exists an integer n such that for all m ≥ n, the submodules in the chain are equal. 

**Ascending Chain Condition**: A module satisfies the ascending chain condition (ACC) if every ascending chain of submodules stabilizes. 

**Well-Founded Relation**: A relation is well-founded if there are no infinite descending sequences under that relation.

9976: Let A be a commutative ring with unity. Let M be an A-module. Let D be the set of submodules of M ordered by the subset relation. The hypothesis that every non-empty subset of D has a maximal element is called the maximal condition on submodules.

9977: Ordered Set: A set equipped with a binary relation that is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive. Minimal Condition: A property of an ordered set where every non-empty subset has a minimal element. Minimal Condition on Subsets: A condition ensuring that every non-empty subset of a partially ordered set has a minimal element. Minimal Condition on Submodules: Similar to subsets, it applies to submodules in module theory, ensuring the existence of minimal elements in non-empty submodules.

9978: Let T be a topological space with more than one point. If T is arc-connected, then T is uncountable.

9979: A Noetherian ring is defined by several equivalent conditions: 1. Every ideal is finitely generated. 2. Every ascending chain of ideals terminates. 3. Every non-empty set of ideals has a maximal element. 4. A subset B of a ring A is an ideal if and only if it is a submodule. B is finitely generated as an ideal if and only if it is finitely generated as a module. The implications between these definitions are as follows: Definition 2 implies Definition 1, and Definition 1 implies Definition 2. Definitions 1 and 4 are equivalent, as are Definitions 2 and 3.

9980: Let A be a commutative ring with unity. Let M be an A-module. A Noetherian module is defined by the following properties: 1. (Definition 1) A module M is Noetherian if every ascending chain of submodules stabilizes. 2. (Definition 2) A module M is Noetherian if every submodule of M is finitely generated. 3. (Definition 3) A module M is Noetherian if it satisfies the descending chain condition on submodules.

9981: Let A be a commutative ring with unity. Let M be an A-module. An Artinian module is defined by two equivalent definitions.

9982: Let A be a commutative ring with unity. A is Artinian if and only if it is Artinian as an A-module.

9983: Let A be an integral domain. The following are equivalent: 1. A is a unique factorization domain (UFD). 2. A is a GCD domain satisfying the ascending chain condition (ACC) on principal ideals. 3. A satisfies the ACC on principal ideals, and every irreducible element of A is a prime element of A. The notation a ∼ b means a and b are associates, i.e., a divides b and b divides a. If a ∼ p1^α1 ... pk^αk and b ∼ p1^β1 ... pk^βk, then gcd(a,b) ∼ d = p1^min(α1,β1) ... pk^min(αk,βk). The ACC on principal ideals prevents the existence of a strictly decreasing sequence of positive integers in the exponents of irreducible factorizations. If irreducible factorization fails, it leads to a contradiction with the ACC. Uniqueness of factorization follows from the properties of prime elements and the cancellation law in integral domains.

9984: Let R be a commutative ring. An element p ∈ R, p ≠ 0, is a prime element of R if: (1) p is not a unit of R; (2) for any a, b ∈ R, if p divides ab, then p divides a or p divides b.

9986: A factorization domain is an integral domain where every element can be expressed as a finite product of irreducible elements. It is also referred to as an atomic domain.

9987: Let A be an integral domain. An element p ∈ A is called primal if whenever p divides ab for a, b ∈ A, then p can be expressed as p = q1 q2, where q1 divides a and q2 divides b.

9988: A ring A is a Schreier domain if it is an integrally closed integral domain in which every non-zero element is primal.

9989:
- Injection: A function f: A → B is injective (or one-to-one) if for every a1, a2 in A, f(a1) = f(a2) implies a1 = a2.
- Injective Object: An object in a category such that every morphism from it to any other object is injective.
- Injective Module: A module M is injective if for every module N and every submodule K of N, every homomorphism from K to M can be extended to a homomorphism from N to M.
- Injective Morphism: A morphism that is injective as a function between objects in a category.
- Monomorphism: A morphism that is injective in the categorical sense, meaning it cannot be factored non-trivially through another morphism.

9990: Let A be a ring. The Nagata criterion states: If S ⊆ A is a multiplicatively closed subset generated by prime elements, then the localization A_S is a Unique Factorization Domain (UFD) if and only if A is a UFD.

9993:
Let \( (R, +, \circ) \) be a commutative ring and let \( I, J \) be ideals of \( R \). The product of \( I \) and \( J \) is defined as follows:

1. \( IJ = \{ a_1 b_1 + \cdots + a_r b_r : a_i \in I, b_i \in J, r \in \mathbb{N} \} \) (set of all finite sums).

2. The product of \( I \) and \( J \) is also the ideal generated by their product as subsets.

9994: A Bézout Domain is a type of integral domain in which any two elements a and b have a greatest common divisor (gcd) that can be expressed as a linear combination of a and b. Specifically, there exist elements x and y in the domain such that gcd(a, b) = ax + by. This property is essential for the structure of the domain, allowing for the formulation of unique factorization and divisibility properties.

9999: Let A be a commutative ring with unity. Let {M_i}_{i \in I} be a family of A-modules indexed by I. The direct product is M = \prod_{i \in I} M_i. The direct sum is \bigoplus_{i \in I} M_i, which is the submodule of M consisting of elements with finite support.

10000: Let A be a ring with unity. Let M be an A-module. M is projective if and only if there exists an A-module N such that the direct sum M ⊕ N is a free module.

8747:
1. Sum of Tangent and Cotangent: tan(x) + cot(x) = (sin^2(x) + cos^2(x)) / (sin(x)cos(x)).
2. Tangent times Tangent plus Cotangent: tan^2(x) + cot(x) = (sin^2(x) + 1) / sin^2(x).
3. Secant Minus Cosine: sec(x) - cos(x) = (1 - cos^2(x)) / cos(x).
4. Square of Tangent Minus Square of Sine: tan^2(x) - sin^2(x) = (sin^2(x) + cos^2(x) - sin^2(x)) / cos^2(x).
5. Difference of Fourth Powers of Cosine and Sine: cos^4(x) - sin^4(x) = (cos^2(x) - sin^2(x))(cos^2(x) + sin^2(x)).
6. Cosecant Minus Sine: csc(x) - sin(x) = (1 - sin^2(x)) / sin(x).
7. Cotangent Minus Tangent: cot(x) - tan(x) = (cos^2(x) - sin^2(x)) / (sin(x)cos(x)).
8. Sum of Cosecant and Cotangent: csc(x) + cot(x) = (1 + cos(x)) / sin(x).
9. Sum of Squares of Secant and Cosecant: sec^2(x) + csc^2(x) = (1 + sin^2(x) + 1 + cos^2(x)).
10. Difference of Fourth Powers of Secant and Tangent: sec^4(x) - tan^4(x) = (sec^2(x) - tan^2(x))(sec^2(x) + tan^2(x)).
11. Reciprocal of One Plus Sine: 1 / (1 + sin(x)).
12. Reciprocal of One Minus Sine: 1 / (1 - sin(x)).
13. Sum of Reciprocals of One Plus and Minus Sine: 1 / (1 + sin(x)) + 1 / (1 - sin(x)).
14. Difference of Reciprocals of One Plus and Minus Sine: 1 / (1 + sin(x)) - 1 / (1 - sin(x)).
15. Reciprocal of One Plus Cosine: 1 / (1 + cos(x)).
16. Reciprocal of One Minus Cosine: 1 / (1 - cos(x)).
17. Sum of Secant and Tangent: sec(x) + tan(x). 
18. Cosine over Sum of Secant and Tangent: cos(x) / (sec(x) + tan(x)).
19. Secant Plus One over Secant Squared: (sec(x) + 1) / sec^2(x).
20. Sine Plus Cosine times Tangent Plus Cotangent: (sin(x) + cos(x))(tan(x) + cot(x)).
21. Tangent over Secant Plus One: tan(x) / (sec(x) + 1).
22. Squares of Linear Combination of Sine and Cosine: (a sin(x) + b cos(x))^2.
23. Reciprocal of One Minus Secant: 1 / (1 - sec(x)).
24. Reciprocal of One Plus Cosecant: 1 / (1 + csc(x)).

5743:
A Cauchy determinant of order n is defined as the determinant of a Cauchy matrix of order n:

\[ \det(C_n) = \begin{vmatrix} \frac{1}{x_1 + y_1} & \frac{1}{x_1 + y_2} & \cdots & \frac{1}{x_1 + y_n} \\ \frac{1}{x_2 + y_1} & \frac{1}{x_2 + y_2} & \cdots & \frac{1}{x_2 + y_n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \frac{1}{x_n + y_1} & \frac{1}{x_n + y_2} & \cdots & \frac{1}{x_n + y_n} \end{vmatrix} \]

Alternatively:

\[ \det(C_n) = \begin{vmatrix} \frac{1}{x_1 - y_1} & \frac{1}{x_1 - y_2} & \cdots & \frac{1}{x_1 - y_n} \\ \frac{1}{x_2 - y_1} & \frac{1}{x_2 - y_2} & \cdots & \frac{1}{x_2 - y_n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \frac{1}{x_n - y_1} & \frac{1}{x_n - y_2} & \cdots & \frac{1}{x_n - y_n} \end{vmatrix} \]

The value of the Cauchy determinant is given by:

\[ \det(C_n) = \frac{\prod_{1 \leq i < j \leq n} (x_j - x_i)(y_j - y_i)}{\prod_{1 \leq i, j \leq n} (x_i + y_j)} \]

or:

\[ \det(C_n) = \frac{\prod_{1 \leq i < j \leq n} (x_j - x_i)(y_j - y_i)}{\prod_{1 \leq i, j \leq n} (x_i - y_j)} \]
